title: Ionic conduction in composite polymer electrolytes at subambient temperatures
abstract:
Ionic Conduction in Composite Polymer Electrolytes 
at Subambient Temperatures 
W. WIECZOREK, S. H. CHUNC, and j. R. STEVENS* 
Department of Physics, University of Guelph, N1G 2W1 Guelph, Ontario, Canada 
SYNOPSIS 
The majority of investigations carried out on polymer-salt systems have been on polyether 
electrolytes at moderate temperatures where such electrolytes exhibit macroscopic unifor- 
mity. Relatively little attention has been paid to the subambient temperature region where 
composite electrolytes based on polyethers exhibit much higher conductivities than their 
pure polyether electrolyte analogues. For all of the composite systems studied the conduction 
mechanism changes from one in which the ions are coupled to the polymer segmental 
relaxations to one in which the ions are decoupled and thermally activated ionic hopping 
produces higher conductivities than would be expected from ion-segmental coupling and 
higher than observed for the base polyether-salt system. This change has been observed 
at temperatures between 10 and 80°C above the respective glass transition temperatures. 
The relationship between this interaction and these higher conductivities at subambient 
temperatures is explored and discussed. 0 
1996 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. 
Keywords: composite polymer electrolytes - conduction mechanism glass transition tem- 
perature 
INTRODUCTION 
Polymer solid electrolytes are still among the most 
intensively studied solid ionic conductors due to the 
variety of their possible applications in electro- 
chemical devices working under ambient condi- 
tions.* In spite of this, the mechanism of ion con- 
duction in these polymeric electrolytes is still not 
well understood. From the viewpoint of application 
as well as theoretical considerations the d.c. con- 
ductivity (adc) is the most important property of 
polymeric electrolytes and, therefore, the tempera- 
ture dependence of adc is the most frequently stud- 
ied? The majority of these investigations are carried 
out at ambient and moderate temperatures, whereas 
relatively little attention has been paid to the su- 
bambient temperature region where the ionic con- 
ductivities of polyether-based electrolytes, the most 
frequently studied systems, are very low. For the 
description of the temperature dependence of ad, at 
ambient and higher temperatures it is common 
* To whom correspondence should be addressed. 
Journal of Polymer Science: Part B Polymer Physics, Vol. 34,2911-2917 (1996) 
0 1996 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. 
CCC 0887-6266/96/172911-07 
practice to use the following (VTF type) semiem- 
pirical r e l a t i ~ n . ~ . ~  
A is a preexponential factor, B is a pseudoactivation 
energy for conduction, To is thermodynamic equi- 
librium glass transition temperature and kB is 
Boltzmann constant. The utilization of eq. (1) em- 
phasizes the role of the coupling of charge carriers 
with the segmental motions of polymer chains. 
Equation (1) has been developed on the premise that 
the mobile charge carriers do not require an acti- 
vation energy to move from one site to the next; the 
“molecular cage” just opens up and a charge carrier 
moves through. In polymeric electrolyte systems co- 
operative motion or coupling of charge carriers and 
polymeric segments is required. 
The VTF type relation, however, does not fit the 
temperature dependence of conductivity in polyether 
based electrolytes over broad temperature ranges!-7 
Essentially, the VTF relation fails at subambient 
temperatures down to the glass transition temper- 
2911 
 2912 
WIECZOREK, CHUNG, AND STEVENS 
ature (Tg). It has been found by Miyamoto' and 
Cheradameg that the temperature dependence of 
conductivity for polymeric electrolyte is better fitted 
by the following equation. 
where, A, is a preexponential constant, B1 
is a pseu- 
doactivation energy for conduction, E,, is an acti- 
vation energy for conduction, and R is a gas constant. 
Other symbols have the same meaning as in eq. (1). 
A similar approach has been and is currently 
being explored by Souquet et al.,5*6 who suggest that 
at temperatures close to and below Tg the temper- 
ature dependence of ionic conductivity in polymeric 
electrolytes can be described by the Arrhenius em- 
pirical form [eq. (3)]. 
(3) 
[Note that eq. (2) is a combination of eq. (1) and 
Souquet et al.5,6 classified polymer electrolytes as 
weak electrolytes in which the creation of mobile 
ions is due to the redissociation of contact ion pairs. 
This led them to conclude that ionic conduction is 
partially thermally activated. The thermal activation 
dominates at subambient temperatures below and 
around Tg, whereas at higher temperatures the 
transport of ions is coupled to the segmental motions 
of the polymeric chains. However, Souquet et al.5 
did not present any measurements of ionic conduc- 
tivity in polymeric electrolytes at low enough tem- 
peratures to provide a real test of their hypothesis. 
It was also previously shown by Chung et al.4 that 
for a variety of polymeric electrolytes at tempera- 
tures close to but above Tg 
there is a deviation from 
the VTF behavior. This deviation occurs at tem- 
peratures where the new temperature dependence 
of ionic conductivity can be described by eq. (3). It 
was also shown" that at temperatures where the 
Arrhenius form best describes the temperature de- 
pendence of conductivity oxymethylene-linked 
poly(ethy1ene oxide) (OMPEO)-NaClO, composite 
electrolytes containing poly(acry1amide) (PAAM) or 
a-A1203 have higher conductivity than the basic 
OMPEO-NaC104 system. Another advantage of us- 
ing composite polymeric systems is their better me- 
chanical stability over a wide temperature range in- 
cluding high temperatures.11~'2 
In the present article, an analysis of the temper- 
ature dependence of conductivity for amorphous 
eq. @).I 
composite polymeric electrolytes is presented. The 
ionic conductivity behavior in the subambient tem- 
perature region is discussed. At these temperatures 
the temperature dependence of conductivity follows 
eq. (3). Using the semiempirical Meyer-Neldel ap- 
proach we calculate the temperature range at which 
the transition from VTF to Arrhenius behavior oc- 
curs for various composite electrolytes. The subam- 
bient temperature conductivities are discussed in 
view of the flexibility of polyether chain segments, 
which in turn, is related to the Tg values. An expla- 
nation for the increase in conductivity obtained for 
composite electrolytes at subambient temperatures 
in comparison with pure amorphous polyether elec- 
trolytes is proposed. DSC and 23Na NMR measure- 
ments are used to support this proposal. 
EXPERIMENTAL 
Sample Preparation 
Amorphous composite polymeric electrolytes were 
prepared according to procedures described else- 
 here.'^^'^-'^ All composite systems were based on 
OMPEO, which after the addition of alkali metal 
salts forms amorphous polymeric electrolytes. The 
synthesis of OMPEO followed that of Nicholas et 
a1.16 Poly(acry1amide) (PAAM), poly(N,N-dimeth- 
ylacrylamide) ("PAAM), 
A1C13, a-A1203 were used 
as fillers. LiClO, and NaC10, were used as ionic do- 
pants and their concentration was equal to 10 mol 
7% with respect to the ether oxygen concentration. 
Conductivity Measurements 
Ionic conductivity was determined using the com- 
plex impedance method in the temperature range 
-20 to 100°C. The samples were sandwiched be- 
tween stainless steel blocking electrodes and placed 
in a temperature-controlled furnace. The impedance 
measurements were carried out on a computer-in- 
terfaced HP 4192 impedance analyzer over the fre- 
quency range 5 Hz to 13 MHz; the peak to peak 
voltage was equal to 1 V. 
DSC Studies 
DSC data were obtained between -110 and 150°C 
using a DuPont TA 2910 scanning calorimeter with 
a low temperature measuring head and liquid nitro- 
gen cooled heating element. Samples (- 15 mg) were 
placed in aluminum pans and were initially cooled 
to -110OC and then heated at 10"C/min to 15OOC.

produces higher conductivities than would be expected from ion-segmental coupling and
higher than observed for the base polyether-salt system. This change has been observed
at temperatures between 10 and 80°C above the respective glass transition temperatures.
The relationship between this interaction and these higher conductivities at subambient
temperatures is explored and discussed. 0

ature dependence of conductivity for amorphous
composite polymeric electrolytes is presented. The

perature region is discussed. At these temperatures

eq. (3). Using the semiempirical Meyer-Neldel ap-

as fillers. LiClO, and NaC10, were used as ionic do-
pants and their concentration was equal to 10 mol
7% with respect to the ether oxygen concentration.
Ionic conductivity was determined using the com-
plex impedance method in the temperature range
-20 to 100°C. The samples were sandwiched be-
tween stainless steel blocking electrodes and placed
in a temperature-controlled furnace. The impedance
measurements were carried out on a computer-in-
terfaced HP 4192 impedance analyzer over the fre-
quency range 5 Hz to 13 MHz; the peak to peak
DSC data were obtained between -110 and 150°C
using a DuPont TA 2910 scanning calorimeter with
a low temperature measuring head and liquid nitro-
gen cooled heating element. Samples (- 15 mg) were
placed in aluminum pans and were initially cooled
to -110OC and then heated at 10"C/min to 15OOC.
IONIC CONDUCTION IN COMPOSITE POLYMER ELECTROLYTES
23Na Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Measurements
Spin-lattice relaxation times (TI) and line shapes
were measured over the temperature range -35 to
100°C using a "home-built'' spectrometer with a
Bruker 6.8T superconducting magnet. In this field
the 23Na resonance w,/27r occurs at 77.0 MHz. Spe-
cial care was taken to eliminate any extraneous
sources of signal which may mask the sample signal.
For this purpose quartz sample tubes rather than
pyrex containers were used. Also the usual copper
resonance coil was removed and replaced by one
made of silver (99.9% purity) because the resonance
frequency of 23Na is close to that of the 63Cu reso-
nance. Other pertinent details regarding the exper-
imental NMR setup are described else~here.'~.'~
In Figure 1 the temperature dependence of conduc-
tivity is shown for, respectively, OMPEO-NNPAAM
(25 mass %)-LiC104 (10 mol % )  [Fig. l ( a ) ]  and
OMPEO-A1C13 ( 5  mass % ) -LiClO, ( 10 mol % ) [Fig.
1 (b) ] composite electrolytes. In Figure 1 a clear de-
viation from the VTF fit is observed at temperatures
slightly above Tg and in this temperature range the
temperature dependence of conductivity can be fit-
ted by eq. (3). Similar behavior was observed for
other amorphous composite polymeric electrolytes.
Over this temperature range the relation between
E, and lnu, [calculated from fitting eq. (3) to ex-
perimental data] is often governed by the following
semiempirical equation [ eq. (4)
where a = l/kBTo and @ = In Ktw,. To is an order-
disorder temperature, Kt is a theoretical charge car-
rier concentration term, and w, is an ionic oscillation
Equation (4) also known as the Meyer-Neldel
rule or compensation law was used to calculate or-
der-disorder temperatures for a variety of fast ionic
including polymeric electr~lytes.'~
Figure 2, sample plots of In u, vs. E, are presented
for a variety of composite electrolytes. The To values
calculated on the basis of eq. ( 4 )  are summarized in
Table I together with Tg and E, data for all com-
posite electrolytes studied. Table I also presents
values of conductivity maxima measured at 0 and
25°C for each amorphous composite system.
In Figure 3 the conductivity data are plotted to-
gether with the inverse of the 23Na NMR correlation
time, T,, as a function of inverse temperature for
the OMPEO-NaC104 ( 10 mol % ) -PAAM ( 25 mass
% ) composite electrolyte. It can be seen that at tem-
dencies of udc and l/rc are similar. At higher tem-
peratures (Tdc increases much faster than l/rc.

(VTF) . On the basis of results included in Table I
it can be concluded that the highest ambient (data
at 25OC) and subambient (data at 0°C) temperature
conductivities of composite polyether electrolytes
can be correlated with the lowest values of E,, in the
Arrhenius region, low Tg values, and high T D  values.
For example, compare the OMPEO-PAAM-LiC10,
and the OMPEO-A1Cl3-LiC1O4 systems with the
others in Table I. These conclusions are similar to
those previously found for semicrystalline composite
polyether electrolytes for which Tn is assigned as
the melting temperature of the crystalline polyether
phase” (T,) and the high conductivity for these
semicrystalline electrolytes below T, was associated
with low E,, low Tgr and high T D  values.
As can be seen from Table I, the addition of var-
ious fillers plasticizes the amorphous polyether
phase thus leading to higher subambient and am-
bient temperature conductivities than for the basic
modified the amorphous phase, which for samples
containing PAAM and NNPAAM is manifested by
the presence of a second Tg close to the Tg of undoped

The increase in the subambient temperature
conductivity observed for composite polyether elec-
trolytes in comparison with the OMPEO-alkali
metal salt systems was noticed in the temperature
range below T D .  Here we suggest that a decoupling
of charge carrier and polymer segmental motions
occurs. This is in good agreement with the recent
predictions of Angel1 and co-workers,21p22
phasized the necessity of decoupling for high con-
ductivity in polymeric electrolytes at temperatures

On the basis of the temperature changes of 1 / ~ ~
(see Fig. 3), which can be related to the local motions
of Na+, it can be concluded that at temperatures
close to T D  there is a change in the ion conduction
mechanism. This assumption is confirmed by the
study of the temperature dependence of wp (see Fig.
4). Note the similar temperature trends of l/rc and
ad, (Fig. 3) and up and adc (Fig. 4) in the region below
ad, increases much faster than either
1/r, or wp and, therefore, changes in the mobility of
Na+ cations or positively charged triplets (denoted
by changes in l/rc) cannot account for the increase
in ad,. Also, according to Angell’s
large value of R, is coupled with high cation trans-
port numbers, whereas R, values much lower than
1 (typical for polymeric electrolytes based on pure
polyethers) indicate a dominant anionic contribu-
tion to (Tdc. On the basis of these results it can be
assumed that at temperatures close to Tg the par-
ticipation of cations or positively charge triplets in
ion transport is much more important than at higher
temperatures where anions or negatively charge
multiplets seem to be the dominant mobile charge
carriers. For example, Li+ transport numbers mea-
sured by us for the OMPEO-LiC104 (10 mol %)-
NNPAAM (25 mass %) composite electrolyte at
7OoC using an a.c. method with a cell containing
two Li electrodes was equal to 0.27 k 0.07. Similarly,
low cationic transport numbers have been previously
measured for a variety of polymeric  electrolyte^.^^
The Arrhenius behavior for the conductivity in the
region in which the positive charge carriers seem to
dominate ionic transport is in agreement with the
pfg NMR studies of Vincent and co-~orkers.~~**~
They show that the temperature dependence of the
cation diffusion coefficient in OMPEO-LiPF6 (5 mol
% LiPF6) electrolytes is governed by the Arrhenius-
The activation energy for wp (=89.4 kJ/mol) cal-
culated for the OMPEO-PAAM-NaC104 composite
electrolyte in the Arrhenius region is similar to the
activation energy for conduction (=lo7 kJ/mol, see
Table I). Similar values of the activation energy for
conduction and activation energy for charge carrier
mobility imply low values of the activation energy
for charge carrier creation. Therefore, due to the
formation of flexible amorphous phase for composite
electrolytes their conductivities at subambient tem-
peratures are higher than for the basic OMPEO-
LiC10, or OMPEO-NaC104 electrolytes. Moreover,
in accordance with the assumptions of Souquet et
al.,596 low activation energy for charge carrier cre-
ation implies either ease of redissociation of contact
ion pairs or the presence of charge carriers in the
form of “free” anions or cations or charge triplets.
Indeed FT-IR  measurement^'^ indicate that the
fraction of contact ion pairs in composite electrolytes
is lower than in OMPEO-alkali metal salt systems.
It should also be noticed that the T D  describes
properties of the polymer matrix rather than charge
carriers because it is similar for composite electro-
lytes based on the same polymer matrix. For ex-
ample, compare data for OMPEO-A1Cl3-LiC1O4 with
OMPEO-NNPAAM-LiC104 [see also Fig. 2(b)] and
IONIC CONDUCTION IN COMPOSITE POLYMER ELECTROLYTES
OMPEO-PAAM-NaC104 with OMPEO-a-Al2O3-
NaC10, [see also Fig. 2(a)] in Table I.
It has been shown that for variety of amorphous
composite electrolytes the temperature dependence
of conductivity follows the Arrhenius semiempirical
relation at subambient temperatures down to Tg
a VTF relation at higher temperatures. The cross-
over temperature between these two regions can be
correlated with the order-disorder temperature, To,
and is 10-80 K higher than Tg.
of TD are associated with low Tg values. Systems
the highest subambient temperature conductivities.
It is suggested that below To the contribution of
positively charged ions to the conductivity is more
pronounced than at higher temperatures. In this
temperature range the conductivity is governed by
the thermally activated mobility of charge carriers
decoupled from the polymer segmental motion
rather than by a coupling of ions with relaxing seg-
ments. The observed results are similar to those re-
ported for blend-basedZ5 and gel electrolytes.26

and Engineering Research Council of Canada via the In-
dustrial Oriented Research Project.
